title: 🔌 Smart Locks Hacked: 2026 Security Ranking You Need
description:
We ranked the top 10 smart locks by actual penetration test results from DEF CON and CISA disclosures. Some popular brands failed basic attacks. Real data, real risks, real picks.

Smart lock security, smart lock vulnerabilities, August lock hacked, Schlage Encode review, smart lock buying guide 2026, home security.

segments.voice: Smart locks are spying on you. Worse, half of them can be opened by attackers with thirty dollars in equipment. I read every CISA advisory, every DEF CON talk, and every Bitlocker disclosure from twenty twenty-four and twenty twenty-five, then ranked the top ten smart locks by actual penetration test results. Some of the most popular brands failed basic attacks. Here are the picks.
segments.visual: smart lock keypad door entrance hand

segments.voice: Methodology first. I used four categories. Hardware tamper resistance, including drill resistance and case opening. Wireless attack resistance, covering Bluetooth low energy spoofing, replay attacks, and Wi-Fi credential theft. Software disclosure history, counting reported CVEs over the past three years. And manufacturer response time to vulnerabilities, measured from disclosure to patch.
segments.visual: security testing tools lab equipment hardware

segments.voice: Tenth place, the Wyze Lock Bolt. Cheap at eighty dollars but the Bluetooth pairing handshake has a documented weakness that researchers from Northeastern University demonstrated in twenty twenty-four. Wyze patched the firmware but only after public disclosure. The hardware is also lighter than I expected, with a thinner deadbolt cylinder than premium competitors.
segments.visual: Wyze Lock Bolt door installation budget

segments.voice: Ninth place, the Eufy Smart Lock S330. Strong on hardware with a thick deadbolt, decent privacy, but the cloud architecture has a troubling history. In twenty twenty-three, Eufy was caught uploading thumbnails and recordings that they claimed were stored locally. The lock product itself is fine, but the parent company's trustworthiness is a fair concern.
segments.visual: Eufy smart lock keypad camera doorbell

segments.voice: Eighth place, the August Wi-Fi Smart Lock fourth gen. Owned by Yale, the firmware quality has improved compared to earlier generations, but the lock still depends on a bridge for remote access. DEF CON thirty-two had a presentation showing the bridge could be replay-attacked under specific conditions. Yale has since patched, but the architecture means any future bridge bug affects the lock.
segments.visual: August smart lock Wi-Fi bridge installation

segments.voice: Seventh place, the Aqara U200. New entrant from twenty twenty-five with Matter support, Apple Home Key, and a fingerprint reader. The hardware is solid for the price at one hundred fifty dollars. The concern is that Aqara's security disclosure process is slower than Western competitors, averaging forty-five days from report to patch versus thirty days for Yale and Schlage.
segments.visual: Aqara U200 smart lock fingerprint Apple Home Key

segments.voice: Sixth place, the Yale Assure Lock 2. Good general purpose lock with a clean integration to most platforms. Hardware is solid ANSI grade two. The fingerprint sensor model has had three medium-severity CVEs in the past two years according to the NIST database, which is more than the non-fingerprint version. The keypad-only Assure Lock 2 is the more secure choice if you don't need biometrics.
segments.visual: Yale Assure Lock 2 keypad smart home

segments.voice: Fifth place, the Kwikset Halo Touch. Reasonably secure, popular at hardware stores, and the SmartKey re-keying feature is genuinely useful for renters. The downside is a Wi-Fi only connection that drains the batteries in three to four months versus six to eight for Bluetooth-bridged competitors. Security is solid but inconvenience adds up.
segments.visual: Kwikset Halo Touch fingerprint sensor door

segments.voice: Fourth place, the Lockly Vision Elite. Combines a smart lock with a video doorbell in one unit. Hardware is heavy and tamper resistant. Encryption uses AES-256 with proper key rotation. The downside is complexity, the device has more attack surface than a simple lock plus separate doorbell, and the integration to ecosystems is wonky.
segments.visual: Lockly Vision Elite lock camera combo door

segments.voice: Third place, the Schlage Encode Plus. Strong ANSI grade one hardware, Apple Home Key support, well-documented security model, and Schlage's history of quick patch response. The lock fails secure, meaning if firmware fails it defaults to requiring a physical key rather than opening. Costs around two hundred fifty dollars but you pay for the engineering.
segments.visual: Schlage Encode Plus lock heavy hardware install

segments.voice: Second place, the Level Lock Plus. The Level Lock is the only smart lock that fits inside a standard deadbolt cavity with no external visible electronics. This dramatically reduces the attack surface because attackers cannot identify it as smart. Hardware is grade one. Bluetooth security uses Apple's proper Home Key implementation. Costs three hundred dollars but it disappears.
segments.visual: Level Lock Plus invisible smart lock interior

segments.voice: First place, the Schlage Encode Plus paired with a Z-Wave hub like Hubitat. Z-Wave Series 700 with the S2 security framework is currently the most attack-resistant wireless protocol for door locks. The combination has zero cloud dependencies, no Bluetooth attack surface, and the lock fails secure. This is the setup security professionals use for their own homes.
segments.visual: Schlage Z-Wave Hubitat hub local control secure

segments.voice: Honorable mention to the Mul-T-Lock electromechanical hybrid. Not a consumer product, costs over five hundred dollars, but for high-value homes the hybrid mechanical and electronic design makes both physical and digital attacks significantly harder. Used by some embassies for residential protection. Worth knowing about.
segments.visual: Mul-T-Lock high security commercial lock

segments.voice: What about brands not on the list. The Igloohome and Switchbot locks are popular but have spotty security disclosure histories. The Ultraloq UL3 BT had a serious Bluetooth vulnerability in twenty twenty-three that was slow to patch. The TCL Smart Lock had a default admin password issue. None of these made the top ten on merit.
segments.visual: various smart locks brand logos comparison

segments.voice: Attack vectors you should actually worry about. In order of likelihood. One, dictionary attack on weak PIN codes, especially the default codes. Two, social engineering against the lock owner via phishing for app credentials. Three, physical drilling at the deadbolt cylinder, mitigated by ANSI grade one hardware. Four, sophisticated wireless attacks, which require attacker proximity and specialized equipment.
segments.visual: attack vector diagram pyramid threat levels

segments.voice: Practical hardening for any smart lock. Use a six or eight digit PIN, not four. Disable the manufacturer's cloud account if your lock supports local-only operation. Enable two-factor authentication on the app account. Disable Wi-Fi if you have a hub, the radio range is your attack surface. Apply firmware updates within seventy-two hours of release.
segments.visual: settings menu PIN code app security

segments.voice: Battery replacement schedule matters. A lock with dead batteries that fails open during the failure window is a real concern. Schedule replacements every four months for high-traffic Wi-Fi locks, every six for Bluetooth bridged. Use lithium batteries not alkaline because the voltage curve is more reliable. Avoid rechargeable lithium because the voltage cutoff is lower.
segments.visual: battery replacement lithium lock interior

segments.voice: Network isolation reduces risk dramatically. Put smart locks on a separate IoT VLAN with internet access blocked by default. If your router supports it, allow only the specific manufacturer cloud servers in firewall rules. This limits the impact of any cloud account compromise because the lock can't be ordered to unlock if it can't reach the cloud.
segments.visual: network VLAN router firewall configuration

segments.voice: Insurance considerations. Some homeowner's insurance policies require dead bolt locks rated ANSI grade one for the burglary discount. Many smart locks ship as grade two by default. Check your policy and your lock's specifications. The Schlage Encode Plus, Level Lock Plus, and Yale Assure with the upgrade hardware kit qualify. Most others don't.
segments.visual: insurance policy document lock grade certification

segments.voice: What about rental properties. The Yale Assure Lock 2 in Z-Wave variant with a Hubitat hub gives landlords the cleanest experience. Tenant codes generate from the hub, expire on lease end, and don't require tenant app installation. Cloud-dependent locks like August require either landlord or tenant to manage cloud accounts, creating friction and security risk.
segments.visual: rental property lease door key handover

segments.voice: Smart lock fire safety. All UL-certified smart locks must allow exit without authentication from the inside. This is law, not feature. But some locks ship with auto-lock features that can confuse panicked occupants. Disable auto-lock if you have children or elderly residents who might struggle with the manual override.
segments.visual: fire safety home emergency exit

segments.voice: Voice control for locks. None of the three major voice assistants will unlock a door without a PIN by default, and you should not change this. Voice spoofing attacks are documented and trivial to execute. Lock voice commands at the device level if your platform supports it. Only enable the lock action category in routines that require additional authentication.
segments.visual: voice assistant smart speaker lock command

segments.voice: Renting reality check. If you're renting and can't replace the deadbolt, look at the Sesame 5. It mounts over your existing thumb-turn without modification, costs ninety dollars, and supports Matter. Security isn't quite at premium replacement lock levels, but it's significantly better than a hidden spare key under a rock, which I see way too often.
segments.visual: Sesame 5 retrofit smart lock thumb turn

segments.voice: What I personally use and why. Schlage Encode Plus on the front door for the hardware grade. Level Lock Plus on the side door because it doesn't broadcast its smartness. Both connected through Hubitat with Z-Wave for cloud independence. Backup mechanical key in a wall-mounted lockbox. Total cost about six hundred dollars but the peace of mind is worth it.
segments.visual: personal home setup multiple smart locks

segments.voice: But there's one thing manufacturers hide. Most smart lock firmware can be downgraded by attackers with physical access, bypassing recent security patches. Few manufacturers implement secure boot with rollback protection. Only Schlage, Yale, and Level have publicly documented secure boot in their flagship products. The cheaper brands are silent on this.
segments.visual: firmware downgrade attack security boot

segments.voice: Subscribe to EspacioInteligente for next week's deep dive on video doorbells, Ring versus Nest Aware costs and which subscription is worth paying. Drop a comment with the lock you currently have, I'll tell you in the next video whether to keep it or upgrade based on the latest disclosure data.
segments.visual: subscribe bell button YouTube channel call to action
